1: Love justice, you rulers of the world. Set your minds sincerely on the Lord, and look for him with all honesty.
The Search for Justice
2: Those who do not try to test him will find him; he will show himself to those who trust him.
The Search for Justice
3: Dishonest thoughts separate people from God, and if we are foolish enough to test him, his power will put us to shame.
The Search for Justice
4: Wisdom will never be at home with anyone who is deceitful or who is a slave of sin.
The Search for Justice
5: Everyone who is holy has learnt to stay away from deceitful people. He will not stay around when foolish thoughts are being expressed; he will not feel comfortable when injustice is done.
God Is Aware of What We Say
6: Wisdom is a spirit that is friendly to people, but she will not forgive anyone who speaks against God, for God knows our feelings and thoughts, and hears our every word.
God Is Aware of What We Say
7: Since the Lord's Spirit fills the entire world, and holds everything in it together, she knows every word that people say.
God Is Aware of What We Say
8: No one who speaks wickedly will escape notice; sooner or later he will receive just punishment.
God Is Aware of What We Say
9: The intentions of ungodly people will be closely examined; their words will be reported to the Lord, and then they will get the punishment that their wickedness deserves.
God Is Aware of What We Say
10: God will tolerate no challenge, and since he hears everything, you cannot hide your complaining from him.
God Is Aware of What We Say
11: So be sure that you do not go about complaining — it does no good — and don't engage in bitter talk. The most secret things you say will have their consequences, and lying will destroy your soul.
God Did Not Create Death
12: Do not bring on your own death by sinful actions.
God Did Not Create Death
13: God did not invent death, and when living creatures die, it gives him no pleasure.
God Did Not Create Death
14: He created everything so that it might continue to exist, and everything he created is wholesome and good. There is no deadly poison in it. No, death does not rule this world,
God Did Not Create Death
15: for God's justice does not die.
God Did Not Create Death
16: Ungodly people have brought death on themselves by the things they have said and done. They yearn for death as if it were a lover. They have gone into partnership with death, and it is just what they deserve.
God Did Not Create Death

1: Wicked people are wrong when they say to themselves, “Our life is short and full of sorrow, and when its end comes, there is no escape. No one has ever been known to come back from the world of the dead.
Wicked Thinking
2: We were born by chance, and after life is over, we will be as if we had never been born at all. Our breath is no more than a puff of smoke; our mind is nothing more than a spark thrown off by the beating of our heart.
Wicked Thinking
3: When that spark dies, our body will crumble into ashes, and our breath will become part of the empty air.
Wicked Thinking
4: In time, no one will remember anything we ever did, and even our names will be forgotten. Our lives will pass away like traces of clouds and vanish like mist in the heat of the sun.
Wicked Thinking
5: Our time on earth is like a passing shadow. There is no escape from the day of our death; it is fixed, and no one can postpone it.”
Wicked Thinking
6: The wicked say, “Come on, then, let's enjoy the good things of life, and live in this world just as we did when we were young and free of care!
Wicked Thinking
7: Let's drink the most expensive wines and use the finest perfumes. Let's not miss a single flower in the springtime!
Wicked Thinking
8: Before the roses wither, let's pick them and wear them in our hair!
Wicked Thinking
9: Let's all join in and leave signs everywhere of our carefree revelry! Life owes us that much!
Wicked Thinking
10: “We'll oppress the poor, even if they are righteous. We'll show no respect for widows or old people.
Wicked Thinking
11: We'll call ourselves right if we are strong enough to get what we want. No one ever got anywhere by being weak!
Wicked Thinking
12: Righteous people are nothing but a nuisance, so let's look for chances to get rid of them. They are against what we do; they accuse us of breaking the Law of Moses and violating the traditions of our ancestors.
Wicked Thinking
13: They claim to know God, and they call themselves the Lord's children.
Wicked Thinking
14: We can't stand the sight of people like that; what they are contradicts our whole way of thinking.
Wicked Thinking
15: They are not like other people; they have strange ways.
Wicked Thinking
16: They think that our moral standards are so corrupt that everything we do should be avoided. They boast of having God for their Father, and believe that only the righteous will die happy.
Wicked Thinking
17: But we'll see if that's true! Let's see what will happen when it's time for them to die!
Wicked Thinking
18: If the righteous really are God's children, God will save them from their enemies.
Wicked Thinking
19: So let's put them to the test. We'll be cruel to them, and torment them; then we'll find out how calm and reasonable they are! We'll find out just how much they can stand!
Wicked Thinking
20: We'll condemn them to a shameful death. After all, they say that God will protect them.”
Wicked Thinking
21: That is how evil people think, but they are wrong. They are blinded by their own wickedness.
Wicked Thinking
22: They have never known God's secrets, never hoped for the rewards of a holy and blameless life.
Wicked Thinking
23: When God created us, he did not intend that we should die; he made us like himself.
24: It was the Devil's jealousy that brought death into the world, and those who belong to the Devil are the ones who will die.

1: But righteous people are protected by God and will never suffer torment.
The Destiny of the Righteous
2: It is a foolish mistake to think that righteous people die and that their death is a terrible evil.
The Destiny of the Righteous
Wis 4.17
3: They leave us, but it is not a disaster. In fact, the righteous are at peace.
The Destiny of the Righteous
4: It might appear that they have suffered punishment, but they have the confident hope of immortality.
The Destiny of the Righteous
5-6: Their sufferings were light compared with the blessings they will receive. God has tested them, like gold in a furnace, and found them worthy to be with him. He has accepted them, just as he accepts the sacrifices which his worshippers burn on the altar.
7: When God comes to reward the righteous, they will blaze out against the wicked like fire in dry straw.
The Destiny of the Righteous
8: They will rule over nations and peoples, and the Lord will be their king for ever.
The Destiny of the Righteous
9: Those who have put their trust in God will come to understand the truth of his ways. Those who have been faithful will live with him in his love, for he is kind and merciful to the ones whom he has chosen.
The Destiny of the Wicked
3.9 Some manuscripts add and he protects his own people (see 4.15).
10: The ungodly, however, will get the punishment their wicked thoughts deserve, because they rebelled against the Lord and ignored what was right.
The Destiny of the Wicked
3.10 what was right; or righteous people.
11: A man who has no use for wisdom or education has a miserable life in store for him. He has nothing to hope for. His labours are useless, and he will never accomplish anything worth while.
The Destiny of the Wicked
12: The woman he marries will turn out to be irresponsible, and his children will go wrong.
The Destiny of the Wicked
13:
All his descendants will be under a curse.
On Judgement Day God will reward the woman who has never been able to have a child, provided she has not been guilty of adultery. Then she will be happy.
The Importance of Virtue
14: On that day, even the man who has been castrated will be rewarded with happiness, if he has kept the Law and has not stored up resentment against the Lord. Because he has been faithful, he will receive a special reward more precious than having children: a place in the Lord's heavenly Temple.
The Importance of Virtue
15: Honest deeds are like a tree that bears marvellous fruit. Wisdom is like a root that is alive and can always send up new shoots.
The Importance of Virtue
16: But children born of adultery or of a forbidden union will die an early death.
The Importance of Virtue
17: Yet even if they do live a long time, they will never amount to anything. They will not be respected in their old age,
The Importance of Virtue
18: and if they die young, they will have no hope or comfort on Judgement Day.
The Importance of Virtue
19: Children born of a forbidden union suffer a miserable fate.
The Importance of Virtue

1: It is better to have virtue, even if it means having no children. Your virtue will be recognized by other people and by God, and you will be remembered for it for ever.
Sir 16.3
2: Virtue provides an example for people to follow; when it is not there, they miss it. It has always been the finest prize a person can win, and it will always be so. It is the noblest of all the good qualities a person can have.
3: No matter how many children are born of a forbidden union, none of them will ever amount to anything. They are illegitimate; they can never lay a firm foundation for themselves, never take deep root.
4: Like trees with shallow roots, they put out leaves for a while, but they sway in the wind, and storms uproot them.
5: Their branches snap off before they mature; their fruit never ripens, and it is good for nothing.
6: On Judgement Day children born of a forbidden union will testify to the sin of their parents and act as witnesses against them.
7: Righteous people, however, will find rest, even if they die young.
8: We honour old age, but not just because a person has lived a long time.
9: Wisdom and righteousness are signs of the maturity that should come with old age.
The Example of Enoch
10: Once there was a man named Enoch who pleased God, and God loved him. While Enoch was still living among sinners, God took him away,
The Example of Enoch
11: so that evil and falsehood could not corrupt his mind and soul.
The Example of Enoch
12: (We all know that people can be so fascinated by evil that they cannot recognize what is good even when they are looking right at it. Innocent people can be so corrupted with desire that they can think of nothing but what they want.)
The Example of Enoch
13: This man Enoch achieved in a few years' time a perfection that other people could never attain in a complete lifetime.
The Example of Enoch
14: The Lord was pleased with Enoch's life and quickly took him out of this wicked world. People were aware of his departure but didn't understand. They never seemed to learn the lesson
The Example of Enoch
15: that God is kind and merciful to his own people; he protects those whom he has chosen.
The Fate of the Wicked
16: Even when righteous people are dead and gone, they put to shame the wicked people who live on after them. In their old age the wicked will be disgraced by young people who have already achieved perfection.
The Fate of the Wicked
17: The wise may die young, but the wicked will never understand that this is the Lord's way of taking them off to safety.
The Fate of the Wicked
18: They make light of a wise person's death, but the Lord will soon be laughing at them. When they die, they will not be given an honourable burial. Even the dead will hold them in scorn and disgust for ever.
The Fate of the Wicked
19: God will throw them to the ground and make them speechless. Like buildings shaken from their foundations, they will be reduced to piles of ruins. They will be in torment. People will soon forget all about them.
The Fate of the Wicked
20: They will come in fear to the Judgement, where their sins will be counted; they will stand condemned by their own lawless actions.
The Fate of the Wicked

1: On that day the righteous, full of confidence, will stand before those who oppressed them and made light of their labours.
The Regrets of the Wicked
2: They will be amazed to see them safe and will tremble with terrible fear.
The Regrets of the Wicked
3: Then the oppressors will regret what they did, and groaning in anguish they will say to one another:
The Regrets of the Wicked
4: “These are the people we made fun of. We thought they were a joke. What fools we were! We thought they were mad to live in the way they did, and when they died, we didn't honour them.
The Regrets of the Wicked
5: And now here they are, God's own children, with a place of their own among God's people.
The Regrets of the Wicked
6: We were the ones who wandered off the right road. We never lived in the light of righteousness; we never caught the first glimmer of its light.
The Regrets of the Wicked
7: All our lives we wandered across unmarked deserts, instead of following the road which the Lord wanted us to travel. And this lawlessness led us to ruin.
The Regrets of the Wicked
8: We were so proud of ourselves — we bragged about how rich we were — and now, what good has it done us?
The Regrets of the Wicked
9: All those things are gone now; they have disappeared like a shadow, like something you hear and then forget.
The Regrets of the Wicked
10: “A ship sails across the waves of the ocean, but when it is gone, it leaves no trace. You cannot tell it was ever there.
The Regrets of the Wicked
11: A bird flies through the air, but leaves no sign that it has been there. It speeds along, riding through the thin air by the force of its wings, leaving behind no trace of its passing.
The Regrets of the Wicked
12: An arrow splits the air when it is shot at a target, but at once the air closes up behind it, and no one can tell where it passed.
The Regrets of the Wicked
13: It is the same with us — we were born, and then we ceased to be. We left no sign of virtue behind us; we were destroyed by our wickedness.”
The Regrets of the Wicked
14:
What hope do wicked people have? Only the hope of straw blown about in the wind, the hope of ocean foam
 that disappears in the storm, the hope of smoke in the breeze. Their hope lasts no longer than our memory of a guest who stays one day and leaves the next.
The Regrets of the Wicked
5.14 ocean foam; some manuscripts have frost.
15: But the righteous live on for ever. The Lord will reward them; the Most High will protect them.
The Regrets of the Wicked
16: He will give them royal splendour and a magnificent crown. He will shield them with his powerful arm.
The Regrets of the Wicked
17: He will go out into battle determined to defeat his enemies, and use the creation itself as a weapon.
The Regrets of the Wicked
18: Righteousness will be his armour, genuine justice will be his helmet,
The Regrets of the Wicked
19: holiness will be his invincible shield.
The Regrets of the Wicked
20: He will sharpen his stern anger into a sword, and the forces of nature will join him in battle against those who are foolish enough to oppose him.
The Regrets of the Wicked
21: Bolts of lightning will strike right on target, as if the Lord had made a bow out of the clouds and was shooting arrows.
The Regrets of the Wicked
22: Hailstones will beat down on his enemies with terrible force. The oceans and rivers will come rushing over them in a devastating flood.
The Regrets of the Wicked
23: Great windstorms will blow them away like straw. Lawlessness will be the ruin of the whole world. Evil actions will cause governments to fall.

1: So then, you kings, you rulers the world over, listen to what I say, and learn from it.
The Responsibility of Rulers
2: You govern many lands and are proud that so many people are under your rule,
The Responsibility of Rulers
3: but this authority has been given to you by the Lord Most High. He will examine what you have done and what you plan to do.
The Responsibility of Rulers
Rom 13.1
4: You rule on behalf of God and his kingdom, and if you do not govern justly, if you do not uphold the law, if you do not live according to God's will,
The Responsibility of Rulers
5: you will suffer sudden and terrible punishment. Judgement is especially severe on those in power.
The Responsibility of Rulers
6: Common people may be mercifully forgiven for their wrongs, but those in power will face a severe judgement.
The Responsibility of Rulers
7: The Lord of all is not afraid of anyone, no matter how great they are. He himself made everyone, great and common alike, and he provides for all equally,
The Responsibility of Rulers
8: but he will judge the conduct of rulers more strictly.
The Responsibility of Rulers
9: It is for you, mighty kings, that I write these words, so that you may know how to act wisely and avoid mistakes.
The Responsibility of Rulers
10: These are holy matters, and if you treat them in a holy manner, you yourselves will be considered holy. If you have learnt this lesson, you will be able to defend yourselves at the Judgement.
The Responsibility of Rulers
11: So then, make my teaching your treasure and joy, and you will be well instructed.
The Value of Wisdom
12: Wisdom shines bright and never grows dim; those who love her and look for her can easily find her.
The Value of Wisdom
13: She is quick to make herself known to anyone who desires her.
The Value of Wisdom
14: Get up early in the morning to find her, and you will have no problem; you will find her sitting at your door.
The Value of Wisdom
15: To fasten your attention on Wisdom is to gain perfect understanding. If you look for her, you will soon find peace of mind,
The Value of Wisdom
16: because she will be looking for those who are worthy of her, and she will find you wherever you are. She is kind and will be with you in your every thought.
The Value of Wisdom
17: Wisdom begins when you sincerely want to learn. To desire Wisdom is to love her;
The Value of Wisdom
18: to love her is to keep her laws; to keep her laws is to be certain of immortality;
The Value of Wisdom
19: immortality will bring you close to God.
The Value of Wisdom
20: This desire for Wisdom can prepare you to rule a kingdom.
The Value of Wisdom
21: So then, you that rule the nations, if you value your thrones and symbols of authority, honour Wisdom so that you may rule for ever.
Solomon Wishes to Share His Wisdom
22: I will tell you what Wisdom is, and how she came to be. I will not keep anything secret. I will trace her history from the beginning and make knowledge of her open to all. I will not ignore any part of the truth.
Solomon Wishes to Share His Wisdom
23: No jealous desire to guard my own knowledge will make me hold back anything. Wisdom has nothing in common with such an attitude.
Solomon Wishes to Share His Wisdom
24: No indeed — the more wise people there are, the safer the world will be. A sensible king can be depended on to give his people this kind of security.
Solomon Wishes to Share His Wisdom
25: So then, learn what I am about to teach you, and you will profit from it.
Solomon Wishes to Share His Wisdom

1/1-2: Like every human being, I am mortal. I am a descendant of that first man, who was made from the soil. I was conceived from the sperm of a man, in the pleasure of intercourse. For nine months my flesh took shape in the blood of my mother's womb.
A King Is Like Everyone Else/A King Is Like Everyone Else
1-2: Like every human being, I am mortal. I am a descendant of that first man, who was made from the soil. I was conceived from the sperm of a man, in the pleasure of intercourse. For nine months my flesh took shape in the blood of my mother's womb.
A King Is Like Everyone Else
3: When I was born, I came into the world like anyone else. I began to breathe the same air we all breathe; and like everyone else, the first sound I made was a cry.
A King Is Like Everyone Else
4: I was wrapped in cloths and cared for.
A King Is Like Everyone Else
5: No king ever began life differently.
A King Is Like Everyone Else
6: For all of us, there is one way into life, and there is one way out.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
7: Realizing that I was only human, I prayed and was given understanding. The spirit of Wisdom came to me.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
8: I regarded her more highly than any throne or crown. Wealth was nothing compared to her.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
9: Precious jewels could not equal her worth; beside Wisdom all the gold in the world is a handful of sand, and silver is nothing more than clay.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
10: I valued her more than health and good looks. Hers is a brightness that never grows dim, and I preferred it to any other light.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
11: When Wisdom came to me, all good things came with her. She brought me untold riches.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
12: I was happy with them all, because Wisdom had brought them to me. I had not realized before that she was the source of all these things.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
13: I was sincere in learning from her, and now I am glad to share what I learnt.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
14: No one can ever exhaust the treasures of Wisdom. Use those treasures and you are God's friends; he approves of what you learn from her.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
15: I pray to God that my thoughts may be worthy of what I have learnt, and that I may speak according to his will. He is Wisdom's guide; he gives correction to those who are wise.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
16: We are under his power and authority — we ourselves, our words, all our understanding and skills.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
17: It is he who gave me true knowledge of the forces of nature: what the world is made of; how the elements behave;
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
18: how the calendar is determined by the movements of the sun, the changing seasons,
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
19: the constellations, and the cycles of years.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
20: He has taught me about the nature of living creatures, the behaviour of wild animals, the force of the winds, the reasoning powers of human beings, the different kinds of plants, and the use of their roots as medicine.
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
21: I learnt things that were well known and things that had never been known before,
Solomon Declares His Love for Wisdom
22:
because Wisdom, who gave shape to everything that exists, was my teacher.
The spirit of Wisdom is intelligent and holy. It is of one nature but reveals itself in many ways. It is not made of any material substance, and it moves about freely. It is clear, clean, and confident; it cannot be harmed.
 It loves what is good. It is sharp and unconquerable,
The Nature of Wisdom
; 7.22 cannot be harmed; or does no harm.
23: kind, and a friend of humanity. It is dependable and sure, and has no worries. It has power over everything, and sees everything. It penetrates every spirit that is intelligent and pure, no matter how delicate its substance may be.
24: Wisdom moves more easily than motion itself; she is so pure that she penetrates everything.
25: She is a breath of God's power — a pure and radiant stream of glory from the Almighty. Nothing that is defiled can ever steal its way into Wisdom.
26: She is a reflection of eternal light, a perfect mirror of God's activity and goodness.
27: Even though Wisdom acts alone, she can do anything. She makes everything new, although she herself never changes. From generation to generation she enters the souls of holy people, and makes them God's friends and prophets.
28: There is nothing that God loves more than people who are at home with Wisdom.
29: Wisdom is more beautiful than the sun and all the constellations. She is better than light itself,
30: because night always follows day, but evil never overcomes Wisdom.

1: Her great power reaches into every part of the world, and she sets everything in useful order.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
2: Wisdom has been my love. I courted her when I was young and wanted to make her my bride. I fell in love with her beauty.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
Sir 15.2
3: She glorifies her noble origin by living with God, the Lord of all, who loves her.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
4: She is familiar with God's mysteries and helps to determine his course of action.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
5: Is it good to have riches in this life? Nothing can make you richer than Wisdom, who makes everything function.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
6: Is knowledge a useful thing to have? Nothing is better than Wisdom, who has given shape to everything that exists.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
7: Do you love justice? All the virtues are the result of Wisdom's work: justice and courage, self-control and understanding. Life can offer us nothing more valuable than these.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
8: Do you want to have wide experience? Wisdom knows the lessons of history and can anticipate the future. She knows how to interpret what people say and how to solve problems. She knows the miracles that God will perform, and how the movements of history will develop.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
9: So I decided to take Wisdom home to live with me, because I knew that she would give me good advice and encourage me in times of trouble and grief.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
10: I thought to myself, “Because of her I will be honoured wherever people come together. The old men will respect me, even though I am young.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
11: They will find that my opinions show deep insight, and those in power will admire me.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
12: When I am silent, they will wait for me to speak, and when I speak, they will pay attention. Even when I speak at length, they will listen with concentration.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
13: Because of Wisdom I will gain immortality; I will live for ever in the memory of those who come after me.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
14: I will hold power over nations and peoples;
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
15: dreaded tyrants will be seized with fear at the mention of my name. I will be famous, as a good king and as a brave soldier.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
16: When I come home to Wisdom, I will find contentment because there is no conflict or pain in living with her, only happiness and joy.”
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
17: And so I thought it over: to be wedded to Wisdom is to live for ever,
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
18: to love her is to be perfectly happy, to do her work is to be rich beyond measure, to share her company is to have sound judgement, to converse with her is to be honoured. Then I was determined to take Wisdom as my bride.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
19: I had a pleasant personality, even as a child. I had been fortunate enough to receive a good soul, or rather, I was given a sound body to live in because I was already good.
Solomon's Love for Wisdom
20: Still, I realized that I would never receive Wisdom unless God gave her to me — and knowing that only God could give her to me was itself a sign of understanding. So I prayed, begging the Lord with all my heart:
Solomon's Love for Wisdom

1: “God of my ancestors, merciful Lord, by your word you created everything.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
2: By your Wisdom you made the human race to rule all creation,
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
3: to govern the world with holiness and righteousness, to administer justice with integrity.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
4: Give me the Wisdom that sits beside your throne; give me a place among your children.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
5: I am your slave, as was my mother before me. I am only human. I am not strong, and my life will be short. I have little understanding of the Law or of how to apply it.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
6: Even if someone is perfect, he will be thought of as nothing without the Wisdom that comes from you.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
7: You chose me rather than anyone else to be the king of your own people, to judge your sons and daughters.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
8: You told me to build a temple on your sacred mountain, an altar in Jerusalem, the city you chose as your home. It is a copy of that temple in heaven, which you prepared at the beginning.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
9: Wisdom is with you and knows your actions; she was present when you made the world. She knows what pleases you, what is right and in accordance with your commands.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
10: Send her from the holy heavens, down from your glorious throne, so that she may work at my side, and I may learn what pleases you.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
11: She knows and understands everything, and will guide me intelligently in what I do. Her glory will protect me.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
12: Then I will judge your people fairly, and be worthy of my father's throne. My actions will be acceptable.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
13: “Who can ever learn the will of God?
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
14: Human reason is not adequate for the task, and our philosophies tend to mislead us,
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
15: because our mortal bodies weigh our souls down. The body is a temporary structure made of earth, a burden to the active mind.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
16: All we can do is make guesses about things on earth; we must struggle to learn about things that are close to us. Who, then, can ever hope to understand heavenly things?
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
17: No one has ever learnt your will, unless you first gave him Wisdom, and sent your Holy Spirit down to him.
Solomon Prays for Wisdom
18: In this way people on earth have been set on the right path, have learnt what pleases you, and have been kept safe by Wisdom.”
Solomon Prays for Wisdom

1: Wisdom protected the father of the world, the first man that was ever formed, when he alone had been created. She saved him from his own sinful act
Wisdom Protected Adam
2: and gave him the strength to master everything on earth.
Cain Abandoned Wisdom
3: But there was an unrighteous man who abandoned Wisdom; he destroyed himself by killing his brother in a fit of anger.
Wisdom Protected Noah
4: Because of that sin, the earth was flooded, but Wisdom saved it again. She guided a righteous man in his flimsy wooden boat.
Wisdom Helped Abraham
5: Once when the nations were frustrated in their wicked plans, Wisdom recognized a righteous man and kept him innocent in God's sight. She gave him strength to obey God's command in spite of his love for his son.
Wisdom Rescued Lot
6: Wisdom rescued a righteous man while ungodly people were dying. He escaped the flames that destroyed the Five Cities.
Wisdom Rescued Lot
7: You can still see the evidence of their wickedness. The land there is barren and smoking. The plants bear fruit that never ripens, and a pillar of salt stands as a monument to one who did not believe.
Wisdom Rescued Lot
8: The people of those cities ignored Wisdom and could not tell right from wrong. Not only that, but the remains of their cities still remind us of the foolish way they lived, so that their failure can never be forgotten.
Wisdom Rescued Lot
9: But Wisdom rescued her servants from the danger.
Wisdom Protected Jacob
10:
A righteous man once had to escape from his brother's anger, and Wisdom guided him in the right way. She showed him God's kingdom and allowed him to know about holy things.
 She made him prosperous and successful in his work.
Wisdom Protected Jacob
Gen 27.43; 28.10–22; 32.24–30; 10.10 holy things; or angels.
11: When others were greedy for what he had, and wanted to take it away from him, Wisdom stood by him and made him rich.
Wisdom Protected Jacob
12: She protected him from his enemies who were waiting for a chance to attack him. She gave him victory in a hard fight, so that he might realize that nothing can make a person stronger than serving God.
Wisdom Rescued Joseph
13: There was once a righteous man who was sold into slavery, but Wisdom did not abandon him. She kept him safe from sin. She went to prison with him
Wisdom Rescued Joseph
14: and never left him until she had given him power over an empire and made him the ruler of people who had once oppressed him. She let it be known that a false accusation had been made against him, and she gave him eternal honour.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
15: Wisdom once rescued an innocent and holy people from a nation of oppressors.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
16:
She entered the soul of one of God's servants and
 stood up to dreaded kings by performing miracles.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
10.16 and; or who.
17: She rewarded God's people for their hardships. She guided them on a miraculous journey. She gave them shade during the day and brilliant starlight at night.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
18: She led them through the deep waters of the Red Sea,
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
19: but she drowned their enemies and washed their bodies up on the shore.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
20: And so the righteous looted the ungodly. They sang hymns to your holy name, O Lord; together they praised you for defending them.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt
21: Wisdom gave speech to those who could not speak; she even caused babies to speak clearly.
Wisdom Led the Israelites Out of Egypt

1: Wisdom brought success to the people of Israel through a holy prophet.
Wisdom Led the Israelites through the Desert
2: They travelled across a desert where no one lived and camped in places where no human being had ever been.
Wisdom Led the Israelites through the Desert
3: They stood up to their enemies and fought them off.
Wisdom Led the Israelites through the Desert
4: When your people grew thirsty, they called to you, Lord, and you gave them water flowing out of solid rock.
Wisdom Led the Israelites through the Desert
5: The disasters that punished the Egyptians saved your people when they were in trouble.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
6-8:
Because those enemies decreed that the babies of your people should be killed, you stirred up the sources of their ever-flowing river and made it foul with blood.
In the desert you let your people go thirsty, to give them a taste of how you had punished their enemies. And then, when they least expected it, you gave them plenty of water.
9: When they were being tested, even though it was a merciful discipline, they learnt how wicked people were tortured when you judged them in anger.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
10: You tested your people, as parents test their children, to warn them. But you judged their enemies like a stern king and condemned them.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
11: They suffered, whether they were near your people or far from them.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
12: Their sorrow was doubled; they groaned as they looked back on what had happened.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
13: When they learnt that their punishment had been of benefit to your people, they realized that it was your work, Lord.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
14: The Egyptians had refused to have anything to do with that man who, long before, as a baby, had been thrown out and exposed; but as things worked out, they ended by being amazed at him. The righteous never suffered a thirst like theirs.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
15: Their wickedness misled them into silly ideas, so that they worshipped snakes and other disgusting animals, creatures without any powers of reason. Because of this, you punished them with millions of such animals,
The Punishment of the Egyptians
16: and taught them that punishment for sin takes the same form as the sin itself.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
17: Your almighty power, Lord, created the world out of material that had no form at all. You could easily have punished those people by sending an invasion of bears or savage lions.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
18: You could have created new and terrible animals, that could breathe fire or roar and send out clouds of smoke, or shoot out fearful sparks from their eyes.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
19: You could have made animals like these that would not have to attack those people to kill them, but could scare them to death just by looking at them.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
20: But this was not necessary. You could have pursued them with your justice or struck them dead at the slightest hint of your power. But you have chosen to measure, count, and weigh everything you do.
God Is Powerful and Merciful
21: You can show your great power whenever you wish, and no one can stand up against it.
God Is Powerful and Merciful
22: In your sight the whole world is a grain of sand, barely heavy enough to tip a pair of scales, a drop of dew on the ground in the morning.
God Is Powerful and Merciful
23: You are powerful enough to do anything, but you are merciful to everyone; you overlook our sins and give us time to repent.
24: You love everything that exists; you do not despise anything that you have made. If you had not liked it, you would not have made it in the first place.
25: How could anything last, if you did not want it to? How could it endure, if you had not created it?
26: You have allowed it all to exist, O Lord, because it is yours, and you love every living thing.

1: Your immortal spirit is in every one of them,
2: and so you gently correct those who sin against you. You remind them of what they are doing, and warn them about it, so that they may abandon their evil ways and put their trust in you, Lord.
The Sins of the Canaanites
3-4: You hated the people who lived in your holy land long ago, because they did horrible things: they practised magic and conducted unholy worship;
5: they killed children without mercy and ate the flesh and blood of human beings. They were initiated into secret rituals
The Sins of the Canaanites
12.5 They were… rituals; Greek unclear.
6: in which parents murdered their own defenceless children. It was your will that our ancestors should destroy those people,
The Sins of the Canaanites
7: so that the land which you consider the most precious of all lands would be a suitable home for your people.
The Sins of the Canaanites
8: But even in this you showed mercy towards their enemies, since they too were human beings. You sent hornets ahead of your army, to destroy the enemy gradually.
The Sins of the Canaanites
9: You could have allowed the righteous to destroy those ungodly people in battle; you could have wiped them out immediately with wild animals or with one harsh command.
The Sins of the Canaanites
10: But instead, you carried out your sentence gradually, to give them a chance to repent, even though you knew that they came from evil stock, that they had been wicked since birth, and that they would never change their way of thinking.
The Sins of the Canaanites
11: Their whole nation was cursed from the start, and though you had not punished them for their sins, it was not because you were afraid of anyone.
God Is Sovereign
12: You created those wicked people, and no one can speak in their defence or condemn you for destroying them. No one can question what you have done or challenge your judgement.
God Is Sovereign
13: All things are under your care, and there is no other god to whom you must justify your decisions.
God Is Sovereign
14: No king or ruler on earth can accuse you of punishing those people unfairly.
God Is Sovereign
15: You are righteous, and you rule everything righteously. You have never used your power to condemn a person who does not deserve to be punished.
God Is Sovereign
16: Your strength is the source of justice. You can show mercy to everyone, because you are the Lord of all.
God Is Sovereign
17: You show your strength when people doubt that your power is perfect, and you punish anyone who knows your power but dares to ignore it.
God Is Sovereign
18: Even though you have absolute power, you are a merciful judge. You could take action against us whenever you choose, but instead, you rule us with great patience.
God Is Sovereign
19: By the things you have done you have taught your people that a person who is righteous must also be kind. You have given your people abundant hope by allowing them to repent of their sins.
God Is Sovereign
20: You were very careful and patient in punishing your people's enemies; even when they deserved to die, you gave them every opportunity to give up their sinful ways.
God Is Sovereign
21: But you judged your own people very strictly, even though you had made covenants with their ancestors and had solemnly promised to give them good things.
God Is Sovereign
22: Yes, you punish us, but you punish our enemies 10,000 times more, so that when we judge others, we may remember your goodness, and when we are being judged, we may look for mercy.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
23: And so you tormented those who were foolish enough to live wickedly — you tormented them with the horrible things they worshipped.
24: They had wandered far away from the truth and worshipped the most disgusting and horrible animals. They were deceived as easily as little children.
25: And so you punished them for their stupidity, and your judgement made them look like fools.
26: It was a light punishment, but those who pay no attention to such warnings deserve to feel the full weight of God's judgement.
27: When they were punished with those creatures they considered gods, they became bitterly disillusioned and recognized that the true God was the one they had always refused to acknowledge. That is why they suffered the final punishment.

1: Anyone who does not know God is simply foolish. Such people look at the good things around them and still fail to see the living God. They have studied the things he made, but they have not recognized the one who made them.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
2: Instead, they suppose that the gods who rule the world are fire or wind or storm or the circling stars or rushing water or the heavenly bodies.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
3: People were so delighted with the beauty of these things that they thought they must be gods, but they should have realized that these things have a master and that he is much greater than all of them, for he is the creator of beauty, and he created them.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
4: Since people are amazed at the power of these things, and how they behave, they ought to learn from them that their maker is far more powerful.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
5: When we realize how vast and beautiful the creation is, we are learning about the Creator at the same time.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
6: But maybe we are too harsh with these people. After all, they may have really wanted to find God, but they couldn't.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
7: Surrounded by God's works, they keep on looking at them, until they are finally convinced that because the things they see are so beautiful, they must be gods.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
8: But still, these people really have no excuse.
The Foolishness of Nature Worship
9: If they had enough intelligence to speculate about the nature of the universe, why did they never find the Lord of all things?
The Foolishness of Idolatry
10: But the most miserable people of all are those who rest their hopes on lifeless things, who worship things that have been made by human hands — images of animals artistically made from gold and silver, or some useless stone carved by someone long ago.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
11: A skilled woodworker may saw down some suitable tree, carefully strip off the bark, and then, with skilful craftsmanship, make from it an object that will serve some useful purpose.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
12: He will take the pieces left over and use them as firewood to cook a meal that he can sit down to and enjoy.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
13: But out of that scrap wood he may take one piece that isn't good for anything — maybe it's crooked and full of knots — and carefully carve it in his leisure time, using spare moments to shape it into the crude image of a person,
The Foolishness of Idolatry
14: or perhaps of some worthless animal. He paints it all over with red, covering up every flaw in the work.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
15: Then he prepares a suitable place in the wall for it and fastens it in place with iron nails.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
16: He is careful to keep it from falling, because he knows it is only an idol and needs help; it cannot help itself.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
17: But he is not ashamed to pray to this lifeless thing about his marriage, his children, and his possessions.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
18: It is weak, but he prays to it for health. It is dead, but he prays to it for life. It has no experience, but he prays to it for help. It cannot walk, but he prays to it for a successful journey.
The Foolishness of Idolatry
19: Its hands have no power, but he asks it to help him — in business, in making money, and in his work.
The Foolishness of Idolatry

1: In the same way, a man getting ready to sail on the raging sea will call for help from a piece of wood that is not as strong as the ship he is about to board.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
2: Someone designed the ship out of a desire for profit, and a craftsman built it with skill.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
3: But it is your care, O Father, that steers it; you give it a safe path through the waves of the sea.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
4: People may go to sea even if they have no skill, because you can save them from any danger.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
5: It is your will that the things you have made by your wisdom should be put to use. And so people can cross the sea in a boat and come safely to land, because they trust their lives to that small piece of wood.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
6: This is what happened in ancient times, when a proud race of giants was dying away. The hope of the world escaped on such a boat under your guidance and left the world a new generation to carry on the human race.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
7: A blessing was on Noah's wooden boat that allowed righteousness to survive,
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
8: but a curse is on an idol made by human hands. A curse is also on the one who makes it, because he works on this perishable thing and then calls it a god.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
9: Ungodly people and these ungodly things they make are equally hated by God,
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
10: who will punish both the things made and the people who made them.
Wooden Idols Compared with Noah's Wooden Boat
11: And so God's judgement will fall on pagan idols, because, even though they are made from something God created, they become horrible things that trap the souls of foolish people.
The Origins of Idolatry
12: Sexual immorality began when idols were invented. They have corrupted human life ever since they were first made.
The Origins of Idolatry
13: Idols have not always existed, nor will they exist for ever.
The Origins of Idolatry
14: It was human pride that brought them into the world, and that is why a quick end has been planned for them.
The Origins of Idolatry
15: Once there was a father who was overwhelmed with grief at the untimely death of his child, so he made an image of that child who had been suddenly taken from him. He then honoured a dead human being as a god, and handed on secret rituals and ceremonies to those who were under his authority.
The Origins of Idolatry
16: As time went on, this ungodly custom became stronger. Finally it became law, and idols were being worshipped at the command of powerful rulers.
The Origins of Idolatry
17: When people lived too far away to honour a ruler in his presence but were eager to pay honour to this absent king, they would imagine what he must look like, and would then make a likeness of him.
The Origins of Idolatry
18: The ambitious artists who made these likenesses caused this worship to spread, even among people who did not know the king.
The Origins of Idolatry
19: An artist might want to please some ruler, and so he would use his skill to make the likeness better looking than the actual person.
The Origins of Idolatry
20: Then people would be so attracted by the work of art, that the one whom they had earlier honoured now became the object of their worship.
The Origins of Idolatry
21: So all this became a deadly trap, because people who were grieving, or under royal authority, would take objects of stone or wood, and give them the honour reserved for the one God.
The Results of Idolatry
22: One thing led to another. It was not enough to be wrong about the knowledge of God. They lived in a state of evil warfare, but they were so ignorant that they called it peace.
The Results of Idolatry
23: They murdered children in their initiation rituals, celebrated secret mysteries, and held wild ceremonial orgies with unnatural practices.
24: They no longer kept their lives or their marriages pure. A man might kill another by an act of treachery or cause him grief by committing adultery with his wife.
25: Everything was a complete riot of bloody murder, robbery, deceit, corruption, faithlessness, disorder, falsehood,
26: harassment of innocent people, ingratitude, moral decay, sexual perversion, broken marriages, adultery, and immorality.
27: The worship of idols, whose names should never be spoken, is the beginning and the end, the cause and the result of every evil.
28: People who worship them lose control of themselves in ecstasy, or pass off lies as prophecies, or live wickedly, or break their word without hesitation.
29: They tell lies under oath and expect no punishment, because the idols they put their trust in are lifeless.
30: But punishment will finally catch up with them, for two reasons: first, they were in error about God when they worshipped idols, and secondly, they had so little regard for holiness that they made false statements to deceive people.
31: When unrighteous people commit sin, they will be hunted down, not by the power of whatever thing they swear by, but by the punishment that sinners deserve.

1: But you, our God, are kind and true and patient. You rule the universe with mercy.
The True God Gives Immortality
2: Even if we sin, we know your power and are still yours. But because we know that we belong to you, we will not sin.
The True God Gives Immortality
3: Knowing you is perfect righteousness. Recognizing your power is where immortality begins.
The True God Gives Immortality
4: We have not been misled by any evil product of human skill, by any useless object painted by some artist, or by any idol smeared with different colours.
The True God Gives Immortality
5: The sight of such things arouses the passions of foolish people and makes them desire a dead, lifeless image.
The True God Gives Immortality
6: Anyone who makes such a thing or desires it or worships it is in love with something evil, and gets what he deserves when he places his hopes in it.
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
7: A potter works the soft clay and carefully shapes each object for our use. Some things he makes are put to good use, and some are not, but he makes them all from the same clay, and shapes them in the same manner. The potter himself decides which objects shall be used for what purposes.
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
8: He is a human being who was himself formed from earth only a short while earlier, and after a little while, when he must return the soul that was lent to him, he will go back to the same earth. He is a human being, but he wastes his labour shaping a useless god out of the same clay that he uses to make pots.
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
9: His life will be short, and he will soon have to die, but he is not concerned about that. He wants to compete with those who work in gold, silver, and bronze, and make the same things as they do. He takes great pride in the things he makes, but they are counterfeit.
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
10: His heart is made of ashes. His hope is cheaper than dirt. His life is not worth as much as his clay,
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
11: because he never came to know the God who shaped him, who breathed into him an active soul and a living spirit.
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
12: He thinks of human life as just a game, a market where he can make a profit. He believes that he must make money however he can, even by evil ways.
The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols
13: This man, who makes idols and fragile pots from the same clay, knows better than anyone else that he is sinning.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
14: But the most foolish of all people, showing less sense than babies, were the enemies who oppressed your people, O Lord.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
15: They thought that all their pagan idols were gods, even though idols cannot see with their eyes, cannot breathe through their nose, cannot hear with their ears, cannot feel with their fingers, and cannot walk on their legs.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
16: Someone whose spirit is only borrowed made them. No one can ever make a god that is equal to a human being.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
17: Every person will die sooner or later, but anything he makes with his wicked hands is dead from the start. He himself is better than what he worships. He at least is alive, but what he worships is not, and never has been.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
18: Such people worship the most disgusting animals, including even the least intelligent ones.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
19: Even as animals they are not attractive enough to make anyone want them. God himself passed them by when he put his approval and blessing on the rest of creation.
The Punishment of the Egyptians

1: And so it was appropriate that these people were punished by such creatures — tormented by swarms of them.
2: But you, O Lord, did not punish your people in this way; instead, you showed them kindness. You sent them quails to eat, a rare, delicious food to satisfy their hunger.
3: You did all this so that the idolators, when they were hungry, would be unable to eat because of the disgusting creatures sent to them. Your people, however, suffered hunger for only a short while, and then they ate the finest food.
4: It was necessary for the oppressors to suffer relentless need, while your people saw how their enemies were being tormented.
5: When terrible, fierce snakes attacked your people and were killing them with their poison, you did not remain angry long enough to destroy your people.
6: This trouble lasted for only a little while, as a warning. Then you gave them a healing symbol, the bronze snake, to remind them of what your Law requires.
7: If a person looked at that symbol, he was cured of the snakebite — not by what he saw, but by you, the saviour of all mankind.
8: By doing this, you also convinced our enemies that you are the one who rescues people from every evil.
9: Our enemies died from the bites of locusts and flies; no way was found to cure them, because they deserved to be punished by such creatures.
10: But even poisonous snakes could not overcome your people, because you had mercy, helped them, and cured them.
11: They were bitten so that they would remember your commands, but they were quickly rescued, in order to keep them from forgetting you completely and depriving themselves of your kindness.
12: No medicine or ointment cured them. They were restored to health by your word, O Lord, the word which heals all humanity.
13: You have power over life and death; you can bring a person to the brink of death and back again.
14: A wicked person may kill someone, but cannot bring the dead person back to life or rescue a soul imprisoned in the world of the dead.
Disastrous Storms Hit Egypt
15: No one can possibly escape from you.
Disastrous Storms Hit Egypt
16: Look at those ungodly people: they refused to recognize you as God, so you punished them with your great power. They were overtaken by terrible storms of rain and hail and were completely destroyed by fire.
Disastrous Storms Hit Egypt
17: The most amazing thing was that the fire burned all the more fiercely in the water, which usually puts fire out. All the forces of nature fight to defend those who are righteous.
Disastrous Storms Hit Egypt
18: At one point the flames died down, so that they wouldn't destroy the creatures sent to punish the ungodly. Those people had to learn that they were being overtaken by your judgement.
Disastrous Storms Hit Egypt
19: But at another point, when surrounded by water, the flames burned more fiercely than fire had ever burned before, and so destroyed the crops on the land where those unrighteous people lived.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
20: But this disaster did not strike your people. Instead, you gave them the food of angels. From heaven you sent down bread that was ready to eat, and they did not have to prepare it. The food you gave delighted everyone, no matter what his taste.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
21: All this showed how lovingly you care for your children. That food satisfied the desire of everyone who ate it; it was changed to suit each person's taste.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
22: It was food that under normal circumstances would vanish away like snow or ice, but now it did not melt even in the fire. This was meant to teach your people that the same fire that was destroying their enemies' crops during the heavy downpour of rain and hail
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
23: held back its power, so that your own righteous people might have food.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
24: You created the universe; it is at your command. All creation uses its power to punish unrighteous people, but it becomes mild and kind to those who put their trust in you.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
25: Creation assumed all kinds of forms to show how you provide generously for all who pray to you.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
16.25 pray to you; or are in need.
26: This happened so that the people whom you loved, O Lord, might learn that they are not fed by what they can grow. It is your word that maintains those who put their trust in you.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
27: The food that was not destroyed by the fire melted when the first ray of sunlight warmed it.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
28: This was to teach us that we must get up before daybreak to give you thanks, and pray as the sun comes up.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat
29: But an ungrateful person's hope will melt away like frost, and drain away like water that is not being used.
The Israelites Receive Manna to Eat

1: O Lord, your acts of judgement are marvellous and hard to explain; that is why people who had not been taught about them went astray.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
2: When lawless people imagined that they had your holy nation in their power, they were themselves imprisoned in a long night of darkness. They lay in their own houses, shut off from your eternal care.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
3: They thought that their sins had been secret and unnoticed, shielded from view by a dark curtain of forgetfulness, but now they were horribly afraid, confused, and terrified by ghostly forms.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
4: Not even the dark corners where they lay could protect them from fear. They were surrounded by horrible noises; grim ghosts with gloomy faces appeared before their eyes.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
5: No fire had power to give them light, and the brilliant stars could do nothing to relieve that deathly darkness.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
6: There was only a dreadful fire, lit by no human hand, that shone on them, and in their terror they believed that the real world was even worse than the things they imagined.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
7: The illusions produced by their magic tricks were put to shame, and all the wisdom they had boasted of came to nothing.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
8: They had claimed they could drive away all the fears and illnesses of sick minds, but now they themselves were sick with silly, groundless fears.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
9: Even though nothing dangerous had actually happened, they were terrified by hissing snakes and animals advancing on them.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
10: And so they died, shaking with fear, afraid even to open their eyes, yet unable to keep them shut.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
11:
Wickedness is cowardly in itself and stands self-condemned.
 Someone with a guilty conscience will always imagine things to be worse than they really are.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
17.11 Wickedness… self-condemned; Greek unclear.
12: Fear is nothing but the failure to use the help that reason gives.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
13: When you lack the confidence to rely on reason, you give in to the fears caused by ignorance.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
14: All night long those people slept the same restless sleep, even though the night held no power over them, since it came from the powerless depths of the world of the dead.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
15: They were chased by hideous forms and lay paralysed as they surrendered themselves to the sudden, unexpected fear that came over them.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
16: People would suddenly collapse and lie locked in the chains of their own fear.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
17: Farmers, shepherds, and labourers out in the countryside were captured by the same inevitable fate and bound in the darkness by the same invisible chain.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
18-19: They were panic-stricken by the sighing of the wind or by the singing of birds in the trees or by the roar of rushing water or by the rumble of falling rocks or by the sound of unseen creatures running about or by the savage roaring of wild animals or by the echoes from the mountains.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
20: In the full light of day, the rest of the world went about its business undisturbed.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night
21: Only those people were covered by this heavy night, a foretaste of the darkness of death that was waiting for them. They were a burden to themselves that was even heavier than the darkness.
Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night

1: Yet all the while a brilliant light was shining for your holy people. Their enemies heard their voices, but couldn't see them. They envied the good fortune of your people, who were not suffering.
Light Shines on the Israelites
2: Those enemies could at least be thankful that the people they had wronged were not taking vengeance on them now, and so they begged them to leave.
Light Shines on the Israelites
18.2 begged them to leave; or begged their forgiveness for their past hostility.
3: Then you guided your people as they travelled through a country they did not know. You guided them with a pillar of fire. It was like a sun that would not harm them on that glorious journey.
Light Shines on the Israelites
4: But their enemies, who were not allowed to see the light, deserved to be prisoners in darkness, because they had made prisoners of your people. And it was through your people that the eternal light of the Law was going to be given to the world.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
5: When your enemies were carrying out their resolve to kill the babies of your holy people, there was one child who was abandoned but later rescued. Then you punished your enemies by killing a great number of their own children. You drowned their whole army at one time in the rushing waters.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
6: But our ancestors had been told in advance of what would happen that night, so that they would be cheered and encouraged by confident trust in your promises to them.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
7: Your people knew that you would rescue the righteous nation and destroy their enemies.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
8: With the same act you punished our enemies and did us the glorious honour of calling us to yourself.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
9: During all this time devout people from this righteous nation were secretly offering sacrifices, giving their word to each other that they would keep God's law and share each other's blessings and dangers. Already they were chanting those ancient hymns of praise.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
10: But their enemies' pitiful cries of grief echoed everywhere, as they mourned for their dead children.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
11: Masters suffered the same punishment as their slaves; the king endured the same loss as the common people.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
12: There were too many dead bodies to count. There were not enough people left to bury them all. In a single moment their dearest children died; all of them met death in the same way.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
13: These people had paid no attention to any warning, but relied instead on their magical powers. But when their firstborn sons were killed, then they recognized that Israel was God's son.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
14: The short night was half over, and all was quiet and peaceful,
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
15: when suddenly your threats were carried out! An invincible word of judgement sped from your royal throne in heaven, straight down to that doomed land. It came like a soldier charging to attack,
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
16: carrying out your firm command with a fearful weapon, standing with feet on the ground and head touching the sky, filling the land with death.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
17: At that moment the people who were about to die had terrible nightmares and were seized by sudden fear.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
18: All over the land they lay half-dead and let it be known why they were dying.
The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn
19: They knew why they were dying, because their horrible dreams had told them.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death
20: Death also came to the righteous nation, for an epidemic struck many of them while they were in the desert, but your anger did not last long.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death
21: There was a certain blameless man who quickly took action to defend them. Acting as their priest, Aaron offered prayers and burnt the incense used in asking forgiveness of sins. With prayers and incense as his weapons, he withstood your anger and ended the disaster. By doing this he proved that he was your servant.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death
22:
He overcame the bitter difficulty,
 but not by his own strength or by military force. Instead, he used prayer to stop the punishment, appealing to the promises you solemnly made to our ancestors.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death
18.22 Probable text bitter difficulty; Greek crowd.
23: Dead bodies were already lying in piles, but he stepped in to hold your anger back and to keep it from harming those who were left alive.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death
24: He wore a long robe decorated with symbols of the universe. In honour of our ancestors he wore four rows of engraved stones on his chest, and your own majesty was represented by the ornament on his turban.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death
25: The Angel of Death was afraid of these things, and gave in. It was only a slight experience of your wrath, but it was enough.
Aaron's Prayer Saves the Israelites from Death

1: But the godless continued to feel your pitiless anger until the very end. You knew what they would do before they did it.
The Suicidal Stupidity of the Egyptians
2: You knew that even though they let your people go and made them leave quickly, they would change their minds and pursue them.
The Suicidal Stupidity of the Egyptians
3-4: While the Egyptians were still mourning at the graves of their dead, they forgot why all this had happened, and they foolishly decided that the people they had begged to leave were runaways. So they chased after them. They were led into this as part of the punishment they deserved, so that they would suffer the rest of the torments they were due to receive.
The Suicidal Stupidity of the Egyptians
5: They were to meet a strange death, while your people continued on their miraculous journey.
God Guides and Protects His People
6: The whole nature of the universe was changed at your command so that your people would not be harmed.
God Guides and Protects His People
7: They saw the cloud over their camp and dry land where water had been. There was a grass-covered plain between the stormy waves of the Red Sea, making it easy for them to cross over.
God Guides and Protects His People
8: All your people, under your protection, saw this miracle and went across.
God Guides and Protects His People
9: They pranced about like horses let out to pasture; they skipped about like lambs and praised you, Lord, for saving them.
God Guides and Protects His People
10: They still remembered what life had been like when they were slaves — how the earth bred gnats instead of cattle, how the river produced huge numbers of frogs instead of fish.
God Guides and Protects His People
11-12: Later, when they desperately wanted better food, quails came up from the sea to satisfy their hunger. The quail was a bird they had never seen before.
13: But violent thunder gave warning of the punishment that was coming on those sinners. They suffered a well-deserved punishment for their great wickedness. No nation had ever hated strangers so bitterly.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
14: Other people had been known to refuse welcome to strangers who came to them, but these people made slaves of those who were their guests and who had shown them kindness.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
15: Any nation will be punished if it does not welcome foreigners,
The Punishment of the Egyptians
16: but these people, who had earlier welcomed the foreigners with happy celebrations and treated them as equals, later made them suffer cruelly.
The Punishment of the Egyptians
17: These people were also struck with blindness, like the men of Sodom who came to the door of that righteous man Lot. They found themselves in total darkness, as each one groped around to find his own door.
God's Miraculous Power
18: On a harp each string keeps its own pitch, but each sound can be combined with others to make different melodies. That is how it was in those days, when the very elements entered into new combinations. Look at what happened!
God's Miraculous Power
19: Land animals took to the water, and swimming creatures came up on the land.
God's Miraculous Power
20: Fire burned even in water, which could not put it out.
God's Miraculous Power
21: And yet the flames could not burn the flesh of the perishable creatures walking in them, and did not melt that heavenly food that would ordinarily have melted like frost.
God's Miraculous Power
22: Lord, you have made your people great — glorious in all respects. You have never neglected them. You have given them help, always, everywhere.
God's Miraculous Power
